figures.caption: Figure 11: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters α2,d for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM with the true values. The results are based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one-factor Gaussian copula with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors.

Table 4: Summary of the prior distributions for all model parameters. The beta prior is parameterised by shape parameters a and b; the gamma prior is parameterised by shape and rate; and the Gaussian (N ) prior is parameterised by its mean and standard deviation.

Figure 3: Posterior distributions of selected GARCH(1,1) parameters and Gaussian copula parameters estimated using the N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM methods for simulated data generated from a one-factor Gaussian copula model with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors, comprising D = 20 series of length T = 1000. Results from N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM are compared with the true parameter values, indicated by the vertical dotted lines.

Figure 4: Selected one-step-ahead posterior predictive densities obtained from N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM for a one-factor Gaussian copula model with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors, comprising D = 20 series of length T = 1000. Diagonal panels show the marginal predictive distributions, while off-diagonal panels display the corresponding bivariate predictive distributions. In the diagonal panels, the vertical lines indicate the true parameter values, while in the off-diagonal panels, the dotted lines represent the true correlation between each pair of series.

Table 9: Factor-specific output heads for factors 1 to 4 in the four-factor, 20-dimensional Gaussian copula model. These layers correspond to the “Output (factor)” components shown in Table 8. The network is illustrated using a batch size of 32.
Figure 9: Training and validation loss curves for the 20-dimensional one-factor Gaussian copula over approximately 1000 epochs.

Figure 7: First (black) and second (orange) one-dimensional convolutional operations with 8 channels. Each channel applies a kernel of size 3 to the input time series yt, for t = 1, ..., 1000, producing the outputs xc, for c = 1, ..., 8. The second operation (orange) illustrates the shift induced by a stride of 2. The figure is adapted from Li et al. (2021).

Figure 12: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters γd for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM with the true values. The results are based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one-factor Gaussian copula with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors.
Figure 26: Posterior distributions of the 1st factor of copula parameters G̃ for, comparing N-IFM with HMC and HMC-IFM for the industry portfolio data.

Figure 14: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters α1,d for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM with the true values. The results are based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one-factor t copula with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors.
Figure 22: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters α1,d for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM with HMC and HMC-IFM for the industry portfolio data.
Table 15: List of industry portfolios.

Figure 18: Posterior distributions of the t copula degrees-of-freedom parameter ν, comparing N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM with the true values. The results are based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one-factor t copula with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors.
Table 10: Training time in seconds for different marginal and copula N-IFM models.
Figure 25: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters ν̃d for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM with HMC and HMC-IFM for the industry portfolio data.

Figure 17: Posterior distributions of the t copula parameters G̃, comparing N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM with the true values. The results are based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one-factor t copula with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors.

Table 11: Posterior diagnostics for model parameters estimated using HMC, based on simulated data from a 20-dimensional one-factor Gaussian copula model with GARCH(1, 1) marginals and Gaussian errors, including the effective sample size (n eff) and the potential scale reduction factor (R̂).

Figure 20 in Section S10 of the online supplement shows the daily stock returns for the 20 industry portfolios. The agricultural industry is labelled as y1. The y1 series has a minimum (-6.42) and maximum (7.66) return and shows the presence of some extreme movements and occasional large shocks. Several other series exhibit extreme movements and pronounced shocks, most notably y7 and y8. The series y1 and y8 shows substantial excess kurtosis (8.23 and 33.16 respectively, compared with 0 under a Gaussian distribution), indicating heavy tails and a higher frequency of extreme observations. In addition, series y1 illustrates clear volatility clustering, highlighting the heteroskedastic nature of the data. These features support the use of GARCH-type volatility models with Student’s-t errors

Figure 8: Schematic of the Deep Sets neural network architecture. Each independent realisation u (n) t is transformed independently by the functionψ(·;γψ). The set of transformed inputs is then aggregated elementwise using a permutation-invariant function a(·), yielding the summary statistic T̃ . Finally, the summary statistic is mapped to parameter estimates λcop by the function ϕ(·;γϕ).

Figure 24: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters γd for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM with HMC and HMC-IFM for the industry portfolio data.

Figure 19: Selected one-step-ahead posterior predictive densities obtained from N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM for data simulated from a one-factor t copula model with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors, comprising D = 20 series of length T = 1000. Diagonal panels show the marginal predictive distributions, while off-diagonal panels display the corresponding bivariate predictive distributions. In the diagonal panels, the vertical lines indicate the true parameter values, while in the off-diagonal panels, the dotted lines represent the true correlation between each pair of series.

Table 7: Neural network architecture of the one-factor 20-dimensional Gaussian copula model, featuring a shared backbone and two output heads in a Deep Sets framework with a batch size of 32. ReLU activations are used throughout, except for the Mean operation (no activation) and the final Linear layer of the lower head (SoftPlus activation).

Table 13: Posterior diagnostics for model parameters estimated using HMC, based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one factor t copula model with GARCH(1, 1) marginals with Gaussian errors, including the effective sample size (n eff) and the potential scale reduction factor (R̂).

Figure 6: Left: estimated correlation coefficients between each pair of the 20 industry portfolio stock returns obtained using the N-IFM method. Right: the 90% prediction intervals over 100 forecast horizons for the four-factor Gaussian copula model with GARCH(1,1) marginals and Student’s-t errors, shown alongside the observed values for selected industry data series.
Figure 21: Histograms of de-meaned, average value-weighted returns for the 20 industry portfolios from 2 February 2015 to 20 December 2018.

Figure 10: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters α1,d for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM with the true values. The results are based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one-factor Gaussian copula with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors.
Figure 23: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters α2,d for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM with HMC and HMC-IFM for the industry portfolio data.

Figure 13: Posterior distributions of the copula parameters G̃, comparing N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM with the true values. The results are based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one-factor Gaussian copula with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors.

Table 16: Posterior diagnostics for model parameters estimated using HMC for the real data application, showing effective sample size (n eff) and R̂, for the Gaussian copula model with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Student’s-t errors.

Figure 5: Selected one-step-ahead posterior predictive densities obtained from N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM for a four-factor Gaussian copula model with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Student’s-t errors, comprisingD = 20 series of length T = 1000 for the industry dataset. Diagonal panels show the marginal predictive distributions, while off-diagonal panels display the corresponding bivariate predictive distributions. In the diagonal panels, the vertical lines indicate the true parameter values.

Table 8: Neural network architecture of the shared Deep Sets backbone for the four-factor, 20-dimensional Gaussian copula model. The backbone maps the input set of copula observations u to a pooled summary representation. This shared representation is used by both output heads that return the approximate posterior mean vector and the diagonal covariance parameters, and it is replicated across all four factors. Only the final factor-specific output layers differ between factors (Table 9). The architecture is shown for a batch size of 32. ReLU activations are used throughout, except for the Mean operation (no activation) and the final layer leading to the factor-specific outputs.
Figure 27: Posterior distributions of the 2nd factor of copula parameters G̃ for, comparing N-IFM with HMC and HMC-IFM for the industry portfolio data.

Table 6: Neural network architecture for the GARCH(1,1) model with Gaussian errors, featuring a shared backbone and three output heads. The architecture is shown for a batch size of 32. ReLU activations are used throughout, except for the Flatten operation, which has no activation, and the final Linear layer of the middle output head, which uses a SoftPlus activation. The kernel size for the 1D-CNN (Conv1d) is 3.

Table 14: Comparison of LPDS values for 100 one-step-ahead forecasts across the four candidate models C1–C4 evaluated on the four simulated datasetsM(1), . . . ,M(4). The values in bold indicate the best model.

Figure 15: Posterior distributions of the GARCH(1, 1) parameters α2,d for d = 1, . . . , D, comparing N-IFM, HMC and HMC-IFM with the true values. The results are based on simulated data from a 20 dimensional one-factor t copula with GARCH(1,1) marginals with Gaussian errors.
